weather: Mostly cloudy this morning, gradually clearing to sunny by afternoon. High 68-70°F with light north-northwest winds 6-13 mph. Just a 10-14% chance of sprinkles early. Tonight turns mostly clear, low 50-56°F. Comfortable May day—good for a walk along the waterfront after lunch.

local_news.findings: Edmonds City Council unanimously adopted the city's first-ever Comprehensive Safety Action Plan on April 28, identifying 11 priority traffic safety projects eligible for federal grant funding. The plan includes improvements to Highway 99 corridors, downtown intersections, and school zone safety measures. Council also approved a $383,456 amendment to consultant contracts for phase two of Perrinville Creek flow reduction studies, partnering with Lynnwood to address chronic flooding issues.

Construction on pedestrian improvements and the 88th Avenue West overlay project will resume the week of May 4, weather permitting. The project involves grinding and overlaying 88th Avenue from 194th Street Southwest, including new sidewalks and accessibility improvements.

Edmonds will begin its 2025 Combined Cured-in-Place Pipe (CIPP) Rehabilitation Project on May 4 to repair aging sewer and storm pipes throughout the city. The project will use trenchless technology to rehabilitate approximately 2,500 feet of deteriorating infrastructure with minimal surface disruption to residents.

Edmonds Police Chief Loi Dawkins reported the department must complete six policy updates by June 30 to access $210,000 in public safety sales tax revenue collected since February. Required updates include alignment with the Keep Washington Working Act, use of force policy revisions, enhanced data collection, and firearms relinquishment procedures. The department is working collaboratively with the Criminal Justice Training Center to meet compliance deadlines.

South County Fire Board of Commissioners will hold a public hearing on May 5 to discuss placing a $420 million fire and EMS construction bond on the November ballot. The bond would fund rebuilding and renovating Edmonds fire stations to meet current safety standards and service demands following last year's annexation of 42,000+ residents into the Regional Fire Authority.

family.choir: Seattle Choral Company has opened auditions for its 2026-27 season, seeking all voice parts for its main ensemble and chamber choir. Auditions will be held May 17-18 at Seattle First Baptist Church with slots available from 9 AM to 5 PM both days. The company will perform Rachmaninoff's Vespers and a newly commissioned work by local composer Karen P. Thomas. Northwest Chorale has also announced auditions for its fall 2026 season, scheduled for May 31 at University Congregational Church. They're particularly seeking tenors and basses for their upcoming "Music of the Americas" program featuring works by Lauridsen and Whitacre.
family.toddler: Edmonds Library kicks off its May toddler programming with "Wiggle Time" storytimes every Tuesday at 10:30 AM, featuring music and movement for ages 2-3. Lynnwood Library offers "Toddler Storytime" on Wednesdays at 10:15 AM with songs, rhymes, and simple crafts. Sno-Isle Libraries has added new Saturday "Play & Learn" sessions at both locations specifically designed for 2-year-olds, focusing on sensory activities and early literacy. The Imagine Children's Museum in Everett has reopened its "Toddler Zone" with new water play features and a soft climber area, offering discounted admission for children under 3 on weekdays before noon.

global_news.findings: President Trump declared the US-Iran war 'terminated' as he faced a War Powers Act deadline, claiming the ongoing ceasefire means he doesn't need congressional approval to continue operations. In a letter to Congress on the 60-day deadline, Trump stated 'the hostilities that began on February 28, 2026, have terminated' despite the US naval blockade of Iranian oil exports continuing. Iran submitted a new peace proposal through Pakistani mediators, which Trump swiftly rejected. The conflict has killed thousands and caused billions in damage while roiling global energy markets.

The US will withdraw 5,000 troops from Germany amid escalating tensions between President Trump and German Chancellor Friedrich Merz over the Iran war. The Pentagon announced the reduction from the 36,000-strong US military presence in Germany, following Trump's criticism of Merz for suggesting the US had been 'humiliated' by Iranian negotiators. Trump has also threatened similar troop withdrawals from Italy and Spain, accusing them of refusing to support US operations in the Strait of Hormuz.

The United Arab Emirates has officially withdrawn from OPEC effective May 1, 2026, in what experts call a 'geopolitical earthquake' that signals closer alignment with US interests. The UAE's departure from the oil cartel after decades of membership reflects its desire to supply more oil than OPEC quotas allow, potentially helping to push down global energy prices. The move gives the UAE greater flexibility to monetize its oil reserves and establish its 'Murban' crude as a global pricing benchmark.

China's export-driven economy is facing fresh pressure from the US-Iran war, with factory orders, costs and jobs being hit hard across manufacturing hubs like Foshan and Guangzhou. Despite weathering Trump's tariffs last year with 5% GDP growth, Chinese manufacturers now struggle with disrupted energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, pushing up costs and reducing orders. Workers report desperate conditions as they cope with both the shift from cheap mass production to advanced tech manufacturing and the new Middle East conflict's economic toll.

President Trump announced he will raise tariffs on European Union cars and trucks to 25% next week, up from the previously agreed 15%, claiming the EU is not complying with trade agreements. The move represents a significant escalation in transatlantic trade tensions and could impact major European automakers. The 25% Section 232 tariffs were originally imposed in March 2025 but had been lowered as part of broader trade negotiations with the EU.

Russia has emerged as Syria's dominant oil supplier despite the new Western-aligned government's distrust of Moscow, with oil shipments jumping 75% to 60,000 barrels per day this year. This shift occurred after Iran halted supplies following Assad's fall, leaving Russia as Syria's primary crude source through a network of sanctioned vessels and ship-to-ship transfers. The trade highlights Syria's limited options as it rebuilds from civil war, though it risks straining ties with Western allies who ended sanctions on Syria last year.

The UK and France signed a new £662 million three-year deal to combat illegal English Channel crossings, deploying riot-trained police, drones, helicopters and surveillance systems. The agreement includes at least 50 French officers trained in 'riot and crowd control tactics' to tackle violence from people smugglers, with £100 million of UK funding potentially withdrawn after a year if targets aren't met. A new 140-capacity removal center in Dunkirk will focus on deporting migrants from the top 10 origin countries including Iran, Afghanistan and Syria.

intellectual_journals.findings: Maria Popova's latest essay explores Oliver Sacks' posthumous collection 'The River of Consciousness,' focusing on how perception is not passive reception but active creation. Drawing on Sacks' neurological case studies, the piece examines how consciousness sieves the infinite stream of reality into personally salient fragments, making each person's experience of the same street walk fundamentally unique. The essay traces how neural 'coalitions' form and dissolve in milliseconds to create our subjective experience of motion, color, and depth, suggesting that understanding between people is les [TRUNCATED]

In a companion piece, Popova delves into M.R. O'Connor's 'Wayfinding,' exploring how physical landscapes literally shape our neural architecture. The essay connects the Bulgarian mountains of O'Connor's childhood with scientific findings that the hippocampus creates 'cognitive maps' not just of space but of selfhood. Notable insights include how the Arctic tern's 44,000-mile annual migration relies on multiple internal clocks—including a lunar cycle detector accurate to 27.3 days—while humans navigate via 'autonoetic consciousness' that binds place, memory, and identity into what we experience [TRUNCATED]

A sweeping investigation reveals how President Trump's administration has systematically replaced career staff with election denial activists across federal agencies ahead of the 2026 midterms. The piece documents at least 75 departures and two dozen new hires, including ten who previously attempted to overturn the 2020 election. Drawing from previously unreported Justice Department meetings, the report shows Attorney General William Barr's 'kamikaze' confrontation with Trump over election fraud claims, and traces how institutional guardrails that failed in 2020 have been deliberately dismantl [TRUNCATED]

A joint investigation with FRONTLINE examines over 300 arrests of protesters during immigration sweeps, finding that federal cases repeatedly collapse under scrutiny. The report documents how ICE and CBP agents have accused demonstrators of domestic terrorism based on statements contradicted by video evidence, with more than a third of cases dismissed or lost. The piece follows Alejandro Orellana, a Marine veteran whose conspiracy charges were quietly dropped after agents found no evidence of the 'secret confederacy' they claimed he led.

intellectual_journals.source: The New York Review of Books
intellectual_journals.findings: Francine Prose reviews two novels—Lee Clay Johnson's 'Bloodline' and Carl Hiaasen's 'Fever Beach'—that grapple with contemporary political violence through dark comedy. Johnson's protagonist Winston Alcorn, a Kentucky man who amputates his own hand in an insurance scam to reclaim imagined Confederate birthright, embodies the fusion of profiteering and prophecy that Prose identifies as characteristic of our moment. The review examines how both novels use regional specificity—Middle Tennessee and South Florida—to explore historical ignorance and environmental destruction as intertwined forces un [TRUNCATED]

wearable_ai.findings: Friend AI Pendant: The device remains polarizing in 2026. Jared Henderson’s widely-circulated teardown (Substack, 27 Sep 2025) describes it as “barely functional, buggy, and annoying,” with 48 battery-status pings in 90 minutes and audio that sounds like “a highly manipulative friend threatening suicide.” Reverse-engineering by Binh Pham (Substack, 22 Oct 2025) confirmed it streams 16 kHz mono LC3 [TRUNCATED]

MagicSchool: January–April 2026 releases move the platform from “helpful tools” to “connected teaching system.” New Studio Mode lets teachers edit AI outputs in a Google-Docs-like workspace; MagicQuizzes now supports district SSO, per-question or end-of-quiz feedback, and auto-generated next-step recommendations; Class Writing Feedback pushes rubric-aligned comments in batches directly into studen [TRUNCATED]

Beyond pendants, the 2026 wearable stack now spans rings, glasses, and shoes. Plaud NotePin ($159) remains the teacher favorite—20-hour record, 40-day standby, HIPAA-compliant, and 300 free transcription minutes monthly. Bee ($49) was quietly acquired by Amazon; existing units still summarize your day but privacy policy now defaults to opt-in cloud storage. Ray-Ban Meta Gen 2 glasses ($329–$499) a [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three major autonomous research systems have moved from lab demos to hardened production in 2026. Paper Circle (arXiv:2604.06170) is now an open-source multi-agent discovery and analysis framework that orchestrates Query, Search, Sorting, Analysis and Export agents to build reproducible paper graphs and knowledge bases; its live site at papercircle.vercel.app already serves the community. Mimosa (arXiv:2603.28986) introduces an *evolving* multi-agent architecture that automatically synthesizes task-specific workflows, then iteratively refines them via experimental feedback; using the new Model Context Protocol for dynamic tool discovery, it scores 43.1 % on ScienceAgentBench with DeepSeek-V3.2, beating static multi-agent baselines. Google DeepMind’s Gemini Deep Think has crossed from IMO gold-medal proofs to professional research: the internal “Aletheia” agent, running in Deep Think mode, now collaborates with mathematicians and physicists to solve open problems, using iterative genera [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional momentum on UAP disclosure has reached unprecedented intensity. Representative Anna Paulina Luna, chairing the House Oversight Task Force on Declassification, publicly claims to have reviewed classified evidence of "non-human origin" objects and promises a forthcoming press conference to present materials once declassified. The Pentagon's All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) acknowledges delays in releasing dozens of UAP videos requested by lawmakers, citing national security concerns. Meanwhile, the Senate has introduced the comprehensive "UAP Disclosure Act of 2025" as an amendment to the FY26 NDAA, which would establish a dedicated National Archives collection for all UAP-related records and mandate expedited public disclosure. President Trump's February directive ordering federal agencies to release government files on "alien and extraterrestrial life" has generated significant anticipation among transparency advocates, though implementation remains uncertain.  [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Medallions, Movement, and Mamdani at MOMA PS1
newyorker.dek: The cab-driving Elcharfa brothers, Bilal and Salah, star in a new piece by the artist Kenneth Tam that showcases the hardships of their jobs, Emma Allen writes.
newyorker.text:
On Election Night, Mayor Zohran Mamdani proclaimed, from a stage, “My brother, we are in City Hall now!” The remark was directed at Richard Chow, a cabdriver from Myanmar whom Mamdani had joined in 2021 on a fifteen-day hunger strike, to demand relief for the taxi-medallion debt crisis. The crisis, in brief: the N.Y.C. Taxi and Limousine Commission sells permits (“medallions”) to operate yellow cabs; the medallion—till recently a near-million-dollar investment—had its bottom drop out, owing to predatory lending and the deregulation of rideshare services. The body of Chow’s brother, Kenny, who’d accrued enormous medallion-related debt, was found in the East River in 2018. Chow would later chauffeur Mamdani to Gracie Mansion.
The other day, the artist Kenneth Tam, forty-four, was in Queens, admiring his contribution to MOMA PS1’s “Greater New York” survey of art with ties to the metropolitan area. Title: “I’m Staying Hopeful and Strong (for Bilal and Salah).” A projected video showed the sixtysomething Lebanese taxi-driver brothers Bilal and Salaheldin Elcharfa performing interpretive movements in an empty gymnasium while intoning words they wrote about the crisis (“no retirement no retirement no retirement / scared scared scared”). Also, on the floor of the gallery: wood-beaded seat covers and other car detritus.
Tam had previously worked with nonprofessional performers in his video-art projects (Craigslist-sourced fraternity brothers and cowboys). But recruiting taxi-drivers proved difficult. He tried pitching the idea from back seats, but no luck. Then a black-car driver took him to the “J.F.K. taxi hole.”
“It’s essentially a lot where all the yellow cabs go and take their breaks,” Tam said. Rideshare drivers, too, but they have a separate zone. There’s also a space for prayer. “Ostensibly, they’re there to wait for the call to go to J.F.K., but I met guys that aren’t even drivers anymore, who go to hang out.” Tam and his producer knocked on windows and handed out flyers. Finally, he approached Bilal in the bathroom line. “I did make it clear that this was not necessarily an advocacy project: ‘I’m not an activist, but I do want to tell your story, in a different way.’ ” A way that would eventually involve dancing with metal folding chairs—but one thing at a time.
Outside the museum, Bilal was parked by a fire hydrant in his yellow RAV4. Salah was still en route from Staten Island. Of first meeting Tam, Bilal said, “He asked me, ‘Are you interested in being in a movie?’ ”
“A loose documentary,” Tam clarified.
Bilal was game. “When someone has anger inside them, you want to explore it with the outsider,” he said.
“Did you appreciate then that you would have to dance?” Tam asked.
“It was really interesting, to go for it,” Bilal said. “Falling down, reaching out, maybe looking for some hope. I was sitting in a box where I just felt like, I’m not able to get out of my situation by circling around, making all the moves—this way, that way, left, right, down, up.” He punched a fist into the air.
When things for the brothers turned grim, “we were always leaning on each other,” Bilal said. “Don’t give up. Keep going.” A friend died of a heart attack when the value of his medallion tanked.
“You’re not sick of each other?” Tam asked.
“We fight. But we never really broke up. The next day, it’s as if nothing happened. That’s our attitude.”
Salah called to say that he was stuck on the Verrazzano Bridge.
Tam said that he’d learned to drive in a suburban part of Queens—“and I distinctly remember, for my road test, I didn’t even encounter a traffic light.”
Bilal laughed. “In Lebanon,” he said, “my dad told me, ‘Just drive.’ ”
Salah was still in traffic, so Bilal decided to drive to him. Eventually, he spotted his brother and honked, yelling, “Follow that cab!”
Everyone piled into one car. Salah recounted meeting Tam: “He asked, ‘What kind of song do you like?’ ” The finished video is minimally scored, but, while shooting, Tam had the Elcharfas dance to their favorite tunes. Salah’s: Air Supply’s “Making Love Out of Nothing at All.” Bilal’s: the Scorpions’ “When the Smoke Is Going Down.”
“I’m secretly hoping Mamdani makes an appearance at PS1,” Tam said.
“He’s going to help, but he’s not going to get us back to what was before,” Salah predicted. Both brothers voted for Mamdani, though Bilal also had a laminated photo of Trump (fist raised) dangling from his rearview mirror.
The brothers’ kids recently watched Tam’s film. Bilal: “They were making fun of me—‘You’re getting old! What are you doing?’ ”
Salah: “My daughter, she’s copying me. She starts making the same movements. I said, ‘Maybe I’m going to be a movie star!’ ”
“I explained to them, ‘That’s an art project,’ ” Bilal said. “ ‘The words plus the movement, it makes a story about our situation.’ Hopefully, maybe, we educate the public.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Known World
literary_pick.author: Edward P. Jones
literary_pick.summary: A Pulitzer Prize-winning novel that reimagines the antebellum South through the story of Henry Townsend, a former slave who becomes a slave owner himself. Set in Virginia, the book explores the moral complexities of slavery through interconnected narratives of both enslaved and free people, creating what critics call "a masterpiece that deserves a place in the American literary canon." The novel challenges conventional narratives about slavery by depicting black slave owners and examining how the institution corrupted all human relationships.

enriched_articles.title: UAE exit from OPEC signals closer alignment with US interests, experts say
enriched_articles.text:
# UAE exit from OPEC signals closer alignment with US interests, experts say

*UAE wants to supply more oil than its OPEC quota and that could help push down prices once the Strait of Hormuz opens.*

Save

Share
